schools.summary: Festus, the new governor succeeding Felix (24:27), promptly went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem within three days — a diligent start (Henry), acquainting himself with the nation's affairs; the providence in his visit occasioning the renewed plot against Paul.

themes: Festus came into the province
ascended from Caesarea to Jerusalem
the new governor's diligence
the providence opening the Festus narrative

schools.summary: The high priest and Jewish leaders, two years after Paul's arrest, renewed their attack — informing against him and earnestly entreating Festus, seeking a favor (v.3) to deliver Paul into their hands; the persistence of the hostility and the corruption of the leadership.

themes: the high priest and chief of the Jews informed against Paul
besought him
the renewed persecution
the persistent malice of the leaders

schools.summary: The Jews revived their plot — asking Festus to bring Paul to Jerusalem, intending to ambush and kill him on the way (as 23:12-15) — seeking a 'favor' that masked murder; the same treacherous scheme persisting two years later.

themes: desired favour against him, send for him to Jerusalem
laying wait in the way to kill him
the renewed assassination plot
the persistent treachery

schools.summary: Festus refused to bring Paul to Jerusalem, keeping him at Caesarea (where his court was held) and inviting the accusers there — a providential refusal that foiled the assassination plot, whether or not Festus knew of it; God's hand in the governor's procedure preserving Paul.

themes: Paul should be kept at Caesarea
Festus would depart shortly thither
the providential refusal
the plot foiled by the governor's procedure

schools.summary: Festus invited the able men among the Jews to come to Caesarea and accuse Paul, if there were any wickedness in him — the orderly Roman process requiring the accusers to substantiate their charges before the court; the contrast with the Jews' treacherous plot.
Calvin: the governor's apparent fairness, later proven hollow when he sought to please the Jews (v.9).

themes: let them which are able go down with me, and accuse
if there be any wickedness in him
the proper Roman procedure
a lawful trial, not an ambush

schools.summary: After some ten days at Jerusalem, Festus returned to Caesarea and the next day took the judgment seat (the bema), commanding Paul brought — the prompt, formal convening of the trial; the diligent governor not delaying like Felix.

themes: he went down unto Caesarea
sitting on the judgment seat commanded Paul to be brought
the formal convening of the trial
the diligent governor

schools.summary: The Jews from Jerusalem surrounded Paul, hurling many grievous charges (sedition, profaning the temple, heresy, as 24:5-6) — yet could prove none; the contrast between grave accusations and absent proof, the malice exceeding the evidence.
Calvin: the providence — the very inability to prove the charges vindicated Paul, exposing the malice of his accusers.

themes: laid many and grievous complaints against Paul
which they could not prove
the baseless accusations
the malice exceeding the proof

schools.summary: Paul denied any offense against the Jewish law, the temple, or Caesar — a comprehensive defense covering both the religious and political charges, firmly asserting his innocence and loyalty (no sedition against Rome); the confidence of a clear conscience.

themes: neither against the law, the temple, nor Caesar have I offended
the threefold denial
the comprehensive defense
the confidence of innocence

schools.summary: Festus, wishing to please the Jews (desiring popularity over justice, Barnes; falling to fear of the Jews like Felix, Robertson), proposed Paul be tried at Jerusalem — a dangerous proposal exposing him to the Sanhedrin and assassins; the corruption of a judge who knew Paul innocent (v.18-19).
Calvin: the swift corruption — ambition and gain overcoming justice in those not guided by the Spirit; Wesley: God overruled it to occasion Paul's appeal to Rome.

debates.question: What does Festus's proposal reveal about his justice?

sides.position: A corruption of justice — desiring popularity over right, exposing the innocent Paul to danger; yet God overruled it for the appeal to Rome

themes: Festus, willing to do the Jews a pleasure
Wilt thou go up to Jerusalem
the corruption of the judge
the providence overruling for the appeal

schools.summary: Paul firmly asserted his right as a Roman citizen — 'I stand at Caesar's judgment seat, where I ought to be judged' — refusing transfer to the Jewish tribunal (Barnes), insisting on Roman justice; the 'as thou very well knowest' sharply rebuking Festus, who knew Paul innocent yet sought to please the Jews (Robertson).

themes: I stand at Caesar's judgment seat, where I ought to be judged
to the Jews have I done no wrong
the bold defense of his rights
the rebuke of Festus

schools.summary: Paul submitted to just law ('if I be an offender... I refuse not to die,' Barnes) yet asserted no one could lawfully deliver him to the Jews (a Roman citizen, Robertson), and appealed to Caesar — the citizen's right of appeal to the emperor, carrying the case to Rome; the providence answering Paul's prayer to see Rome (Romans 1:10, Barnes).

Calvin: the lawful use of the magistrate's help — God, who appointed judgment-seats, grants liberty to his people to use them lawfully; Paul not evading justice but seeking it before the highest court.

themes: if I be an offender, I refuse not to die
no man may deliver me unto them
I appeal unto Caesar
the lawful appeal and the providence of Rome

schools.summary: Festus, having conferred with his council (his assessors, Robertson), granted the appeal — 'unto Caesar shalt thou go' — relieved to be rid of the troublesome case (Barnes), perhaps with a sneer knowing what kind of judge Nero was (Ellicott); the journey to Rome now certain.

themes: when he had conferred with the council
unto Caesar shalt thou go
the granting of the appeal
the journey to Rome assured

schools.summary: Agrippa (Herod Agrippa II, the last Herodian king) and Bernice his sister (their relationship scandalous, occasioning suspicion of incest) came to Caesarea to salute the new governor — a diplomatic courtesy call; the providential occasion leading to Paul's defense before Agrippa (ch. 26).

themes: king Agrippa and Bernice came unto Caesarea
to salute Festus
the last Herodian king and his sister
the providential occasion

schools.summary: After several days, Festus laid Paul's case before Agrippa — seeking the knowledgeable king's help with his perplexity about what to write to Caesar (v.26) — noting Paul had been left in bonds by Felix (24:27), displaying the long unjust custody.

themes: Festus declared Paul's cause unto the king
a certain man left in bonds by Felix
the governor consulting Agrippa
the inheritance of injustice

schools.summary: Festus recounts how the chief priests and elders at Jerusalem informed against Paul, demanding a verdict of condemnation — seeking not justice but Paul's condemnation (even death), revealing their prejudice; the contrast with Roman procedure (v.16).

themes: the chief priests and elders informed me
desiring to have judgment against him
the demand for condemnation
the Jews' prejudice

schools.summary: Festus cites the Roman principle — no man condemned without confronting his accusers and having opportunity to defend himself (Clarke: condemn no man unheard, a sacred maxim) — refusing the Jews' demand for condemnation without trial; the principle providentially protecting Paul.

themes: it is not the manner of the Romans to deliver any man to die
the accusers face to face, licence to answer
the Roman principle of justice
condemn no man unheard

schools.summary: Festus recounts his prompt procedure — without delay, the next day, he took the judgment seat and commanded Paul brought (as v.6) — presenting himself to Agrippa as a diligent, fair judge, contrasting with the Jews' demand for summary condemnation.

themes: without any delay I sat on the judgment seat
commanded the man to be brought forth
Festus's prompt procedure
the diligent judge

schools.summary: Festus expected grave charges (a crime against Rome, given the long custody and the Jews' violence, Barnes) but was surprised the accusers brought no real crime (Clarke: scarcely anything amounting to a crime), only religious disputes; the governor's surprise testifying to Paul's innocence.
Calvin: the providence — the very emptiness of the charges vindicated Paul.

themes: the accusers brought none accusation of such things as I supposed
the surprise at the baseless charges
the vindication of Paul
the governor's confession of innocence

schools.summary: Festus described the dispute as concerning 'their own superstition' (the neutral term for religion, not to insult Agrippa, Barnes, Robertson) and 'one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive' — supercilious scorn (Robertson), yet correctly stating the central point of Paul's preaching, that Jesus lives; a Roman magistrate's dismissal of the glorious truth (Barnes).
Wesley laments Festus's coldness — mentioning the things of last importance so coldly, doubting only because he would not search into the evidence of the resurrection.

debates.question: What does Festus's description reveal about the gospel and unbelief?

sides.position: The worldling's contemptuous dismissal of the resurrection as a trivial 'question' — yet he correctly names the heart of Paul's preaching, that Jesus lives

themes: certain questions of their own superstition
one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive
the pagan-eye view of the resurrection
the centrality of the risen Christ

schools.summary: Festus, perplexed about the religious questions (in doubt which way to turn, Robertson), proposed Paul be tried at Jerusalem — framing it as motivated by perplexity, though Luke earlier noted his desire to please the Jews (v.9), both motives perhaps operating; his perplexity justifying consulting Agrippa (Ellicott).

themes: because I doubted of such manner of questions
whether he would go to Jerusalem
the governor's perplexity
the favorable account of his proposal

schools.summary: Festus recounts that Paul appealed to be kept for the hearing of Augustus (the imperial title, here Nero, Robertson, Ellicott) — a legal examination before the emperor (diagnosis, Robertson) — so Festus kept him to send to Caesar; the appeal setting Paul's course for Rome and creating Festus's dilemma.

themes: Paul had appealed to be reserved unto the hearing of Augustus
I commanded him to be kept till I send him to Caesar
the imperial title
the appeal and Festus's predicament

schools.summary: Agrippa wished to hear Paul — induced by curiosity (having heard of Jesus and the Christians, Barnes; like Herod's curiosity, Luke 23:8, Clarke) — and Festus promised he should hear him next day; this giving occasion to Paul's noblest defense (ch. 26, Barnes).
Calvin: the cold curiosity yet the providential fruit — even if none believed sincerely, the testimony confirmed the godly and shamed the enemies.

themes: I would also hear the man myself
thou shalt hear him
Agrippa's curiosity
the providential occasion of the great defense

schools.summary: Agrippa and Bernice came with great pomp (royal display, retinue), entering the hall with the chief captains and principal men of Caesarea, and Paul was brought forth — a grand audience for the apostle's testimony; the providence in the splendid assembly gathered to hear the gospel.
Chrysostom: the contrast — the worldly pomp of the king set against the chained apostle who possessed the truth, earthly grandeur against spiritual riches.

themes: Agrippa and Bernice came with great pomp
into the place of hearing with the chief captains
the grand audience
worldly pomp vs the chained apostle

schools.summary: Festus introduced Paul's case to Agrippa and the assembly — the whole multitude of the Jews, at Jerusalem and Caesarea, had demanded Paul's death, crying he ought not to live; the vehemence of the persecution against the absence of any real crime (v.18).

themes: ye see this man, about whom all the multitude of the Jews have dealt with me
crying that he ought not to live
the demand for Paul's death
Festus introducing the case

schools.summary: Festus confessed he found Paul guilty of nothing worthy of death (like every Roman magistrate — Gallio, Lysias, Felix) and, since Paul had appealed to Augustus, determined to send him to Rome; the confession of innocence vindicating Paul, the appeal necessitating the journey to Caesar.

themes: I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death
he himself hath appealed to Augustus
the confession of innocence
the decision to send Paul to Rome

schools.summary: Festus confessed he had no certain charge to write to 'my lord' — the absurdity of sending a prisoner without a charge (Barnes) — and brought Paul before Agrippa hoping the king's examination would yield something to write; 'my lord' (Dominus/Kyrios) refused by Augustus and Tiberius but accepted by Nero, reflecting emperor-worship (Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott).

themes: I have no certain thing to write unto my lord
brought him before thee, O king Agrippa
the imperial title and emperor-worship
the governor's predicament

schools.summary: Festus admitted it unreasonable to send a prisoner without specifying the charges (Barnes) — hence the hearing before Agrippa to find something to write; Barnes draws the lessons: Agrippa's curiosity, Festus's worldly indifference (never asking whether Jesus's being alive made his religion true), and Paul's earnest devotion.
Clarke: the lawful use of civil privileges in God's cause — from Paul's appeal we learn it is lawful to avail ourselves of the civil privileges God's mercy has given.

themes: unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not signify the crimes
the governor's reasonable admission
the lessons: curiosity, indifference, devotion
the lawful use of civil privileges
